claims.claim: Healthy people can include up to one whole egg daily in their diets, while older people with healthy cholesterol levels may have two.

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Maximum daily allowance for healthy people
claim_quantities.source_quote: up to a whole egg... each day

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Maximum for older people with healthy levels
claim_quantities.source_quote: older people with healthy cholesterol levels can have two

claims.importance_rationale: Provides specific guidance on egg consumption for the general healthy population.
claims.relevance_rationale: Provides specific guidance on egg consumption for the general healthy population.
claims.source_quote: The 2019 science advisory says healthy people can include up to a whole egg or the equivalent in their diets each day; given the nutritional benefits and convenience, older people with healthy cholesterol levels can have two.

claim_context.population: Healthy people; older people with healthy cholesterol levels
claim_context.stated_dose_or_threshold: one whole egg (daily); two (for older people)

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Maximum daily allowance for healthy people
claim_quantities.source_quote: up to a whole egg... each day

claim_quantities.local_interpretation: Maximum for older people with healthy levels
claim_quantities.source_quote: older people with healthy cholesterol levels can have two

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Healthy adults can eat up to one egg a day, and older adults with healthy levels may have two.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: For healthy adults with normal cholesterol levels, a few eggs per week are considered tolerable as part of a balanced diet, but individuals with high LDL or other risk factors should limit both saturated fat and dietary cholesterol.

supporting_quotes.quote: The 2019 science advisory says healthy people can include up to a whole egg or the equivalent in their diets each day; given the nutritional benefits and convenience, older people with healthy cholesterol levels can have two.

claims.claim: Individuals with high LDL cholesterol or other risk factors (overweight, obesity) should reduce sources of both saturated fat and dietary cholesterol.
claims.importance_rationale: Identifies the specific population where egg/cholesterol restriction is necessary.
claims.relevance_rationale: Identifies the specific population where egg/cholesterol restriction is necessary.
claims.source_quote: Anyone with a high LDL cholesterol level should consider reducing sources of both saturated fat and dietary cholesterol, Van Horn said, because together they are considered more likely to contribute to arterial plaque. This is especially a concern among people with overweight, obesity or other risk factors.

claim_context.population: People with high LDL cholesterol; people with overweight, obesity or other risk factors

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: People with high LDL or obesity should limit both saturated fat and dietary cholesterol.
whole_doc_source_card.source_bottom_line: For healthy adults with normal cholesterol levels, a few eggs per week are considered tolerable as part of a balanced diet, but individuals with high LDL or other risk factors should limit both saturated fat and dietary cholesterol.

supporting_quotes.quote: Anyone with a high LDL cholesterol level should consider reducing sources of both saturated fat and dietary cholesterol, Van Horn said, because together they are considered more likely to contribute to arterial plaque. This is especially a concern among people with overweight, obesity or other risk factors.

claims.claim: Studies have not generally supported an association between dietary cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.
claims.importance_rationale: Provides the scientific basis for why current guidelines are less restrictive than historical ones.
claims.relevance_rationale: Provides the scientific basis for why current guidelines are less restrictive than historical ones.
claims.source_quote: the 2019 science advisory said studies have not generally supported an association between dietary cholesterol and cardiovascular risk.

whole_doc_source_card.natural_bottom_line: Current research does not generally link dietary cholesterol to heart disease risk.
